mnemonics.title: SLA Components
mnemonics.content: MADE VIP: Metrics, Availability, Downtime credits, Escalation, Violation remedies, Infrastructure scope, Performance benchmarks

mnemonics.title: SLA Lifecycle Phases
mnemonics.content: DAN IMR: Define, Agree, Negotiate, Implement, Monitor, Report, Review (or Renewal)

acronyms.usage: Contract defining service standards and remedies between provider and customer
Internal performance target, often stricter than SLA, used by operations teams
Maximum acceptable time to restore service after disruption
Maximum acceptable data loss measured in time (e.g., can lose 1 hour of data)
Average time between system breakdowns, indicating reliability
Average time to restore service after failure
Often required alongside SLA for confidentiality of metrics and incidents

visualTips.visualization: Think of a ladder: Each '9' is a step up. 99% (2 nines) = step 2, 99.9% (3 nines) = step 3. Higher = more reliability, less downtime allowed. Visualize a clock face showing minutes: 3 nines = ~43 min/month = small slice; 5 nines = ~26 sec/month = almost invisible dot.
Imagine a layered cake: Bottom layer (physical) = provider; Middle layers (network, storage, OS) = shared; Top layer (data, apps) = customer. More service model = higher the layer provider manages.
Think of SLA as the finish line in a contract (must achieve); SLO as your training goal (aim for better). SLO is like a personal best, SLA is the qualifying time.
Picture a sliding scale: The more you fall below SLA target, the higher the credit percentage. 95% achieved vs 99% target = 10% credit; 90% = 25% credit. Like progressive penalty in tax brackets.

analogies.analogy: Just like car insurance defines what is covered and what happens when something goes wrong, an SLA defines what service levels are guaranteed and what compensation you get when the provider fails.
Speed limit signs tell you the maximum allowed speed; SLA metrics tell you the minimum guaranteed performance. Exceeding speed limits has penalties; not meeting SLA has service credits.
Fast food (IaaS): you get ingredients, cook yourself. Fast-casual (PaaS): they prep, you assemble. Fine dining (SaaS): everything prepared and served. In each, who does what varies.
99% = bronze (good), 99.9% = silver (very good), 99.99% = gold (excellent), 99.999% = platinum (exceptional). Each nine is like moving up a medal level in reliability.
